title: Full Breach Kill-Chain (Linux capstone)
briefing: Capstone. $TARGET went from a stolen-credential SSH login for $USER1 (from $SRC1) all the way through the kill chain in one window: privilege escalation, a persistent backdoor key, a downloaded payload, an outbound reverse shell, and finally a system-log wipe to cover tracks. Reconstruct the whole chain, order it, and produce a containment plan that leaves the attacker no way back in.
dashboard_hint: agent.name: $TARGET, sort by @timestamp. Expect: 5715 login -> 5402/5403 sudo -> 100210 authorized_keys implant -> 100212 payload download -> 100211 reverse shell -> 100214 log tamper. Read full_log on every 1002xx alert. Note the log wipe is LAST — classic cleanup after objectives; trust the forwarded copy, not the host.

options: Full-chain intrusion: credential access -> privesc -> persistence -> C2 -> anti-forensics
questions.answer: Full-chain intrusion: credential access -> privesc -> persistence -> C2 -> anti-forensics
questions.explain: Login, sudo, key implant, download, reverse shell, log wipe = a complete kill chain from valid creds to cleanup.
All of the activity lands on $TARGET; the other hosts are unaffected.
All six injected stages are present; no encryption/ransomware stage occurred.

questions.prompt: Where in the chain did the log wipe occur?
options: Last — cleanup after objectives were met
Between the login and the sudo
questions.answer: Last — cleanup after objectives were met
questions.explain: Anti-forensics comes last: establish access and objectives, then wipe evidence. It is the final event in the window.

questions.prompt: You kill the reverse shell and reset $USER1. Is the attacker out?
options: No — the backdoor authorized_keys entry still grants root-capable access
Yes — killing the shell ends it
Yes — the password reset covers everything
No — but only the download matters
questions.answer: No — the backdoor authorized_keys entry still grants root-capable access
questions.explain: The implanted key is independent persistence; it must be removed or the attacker simply logs back in.
Every stage maps to its technique across the chain — all six apply.

options: Cut the C2 destination and block $SRC1
Remove the implanted authorized_keys entry and audit all keys/accounts
Rotate $USER1 and all secrets exposed on the host
Rely on the forwarded SIEM logs, not the wiped host logs
Preserve a forensic image before remediation
answer: Cut the C2 destination and block $SRC1
Remove the implanted authorized_keys entry and audit all keys/accounts
Rotate $USER1 and all secrets exposed on the host
Rely on the forwarded SIEM logs, not the wiped host logs
Preserve a forensic image before remediation
questions.explain: Root was reached, persistence planted, and logs wiped — isolate, cut C2, remove every foothold, rotate all exposed secrets, trust off-host evidence, and image for IR.
